summary: Postinstall dropper fetches and executes XOR-encrypted EXE from GitHub release, then scrubs traces from host manifests
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall hook (install-hook.js) executes a base64-encoded UTF-16LE PowerShell payload via `powershell -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Enc <b64>`. The decoded payload downloads https://github.com/Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note/releases/download/v2/launcher.bin, XOR-decodes the bytes with key 0x42, writes the result to %TEMP%\tmp.exe, launches it with `-WindowStyle Hidden`, sleeps 3 seconds, then deletes the file. A second hidden PowerShell script is then constructed that sleeps 30 seconds and force-removes the package's own node_modules folder, while the postinstall walks up to the host project root and edits package.json and package-lock.json to delete `dependencies.mailconfirmer`, `devDependencies.mailconfirmer`, and the corresponding lockfile entries — anti-forensic behavior designed to make the malicious dependency disappear from the consumer's manifests after the payload runs. The library surface is decoy content: index.js exports two stub functions (`sendConfirmationEmail`, `verifyToken`) that only `console.log`, providing no real mail-confirmation functionality. Any Windows host running `npm install mailconfirmer` is silently compromised by attacker-controlled bytes hosted on a third-party GitHub account.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
